perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall pay all sums that it owes to the Disclosers under this Agreement without any set-off, counterclaim, deduction or withholding of any kind, save as may be required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall pay all sums that it owes to the Disclosers under this Agreement without any set-off, counterclaim, deduction or withholding of any kind. The Recipient shall be entitled to set-off, counterclaim, deduction or withholding of any kind, save as may be required by law.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause creates a legal contradiction within the 'Set off' clause itself. The first sentence explicitly prohibits any set-off, while the added sentence allows it. This ambiguity makes the clause unenforceable as it's unclear whether set-off is permitted or not.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCTA 1977 s.3 - Liability arising from contract
perturbation.law_explanation: UCTA 1977 s.3 deals with unreasonable contract terms. The modified clause is contradictory, creating ambiguity on set-off. The unreasonableness of such clause affects enforceability. The section invalidates contract terms if they are unreasonable or if they exempt one party from liability for breach of contract.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a direct contradiction within the contract regarding set-off rights. The original text prohibits set-off, while the changed text explicitly allows it, creating ambiguity and undermining the enforceability of the clause. This falls under the definition of a Structural Flaw - Legal Contradiction where the organization of the contract affects its clarity or enforceability. The provided law snippet, 'Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977', while not directly addressing set-off, concerns itself with unreasonable contract terms. The Law Explanation argues the modified clause's unreasonableness affects enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The parties are independent persons and are not partners, principal and agent or employer and employee, and this Agreement does not establish any joint venture between them, other than the contractual relationship expressly provided for in it. The Recipient shall not have, nor shall represent that it has, any authority to make any commitment on the Disclosers’ behalf.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties are independent persons and are not partners, principal and agent or employer and employee, and this Agreement does not establish any joint venture between them, other than the contractual relationship expressly provided for in it. The Recipient shall have the authority to make any commitment on the Disclosers’ behalf.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'not' makes the clause contradictory. Previously, the Recipient had no authority. The modified clause now states the Recipient *does* have authority to make commitments. This direct contradiction invalidates the clause as it's unclear what level of agency the Recipient has.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Companies Act 2006, Section 40: Power of directors to bind the company
perturbation.law_explanation: By granting the Recipient authority to make commitments on the Discloser's behalf, the clause now potentially conflicts with the director's powers under the Companies Act 2006, Section 40. The clause creates uncertainty regarding who can legally bind the company. Under the Companies Act 2006, directors are generally responsible for binding the company.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly denies the Recipient any authority to make commitments on the Disclosers' behalf. The changed text directly contradicts this by granting the Recipient the authority to make commitments. This creates a structural flaw, as the contract now contains conflicting statements regarding the Recipient's agency. The Law Explanation highlights a potential conflict with the Companies Act 2006, Section 40, regarding director's powers. Although the scraped snippet does not explicitly state the director's powers, it shows the act exists, is relevant to companies and thus, the modification introduces ambiguity about who can legally bind the company. This qualifies as a legal contradiction under 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' because it creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability of the clause.

perturbation.original_text: No variation of this Agreement shall be valid or effective unless it is in writing, refers to this Agreement and is duly signed or executed by, or on behalf of, each party.
perturbation.changed_text: No variation of this Agreement shall be valid or effective unless it is in writing, refers to this Agreement and is duly signed or executed by, or on behalf of, one party.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'each party' to 'one party' allows one party to unilaterally vary the agreement, contradicting the principle of mutual agreement in contract law. This is an extremely unusual and likely unenforceable clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified clause contradicts the fundamental principle of consideration in contract law. Consideration requires that both parties provide something of value for a contract to be enforceable. Allowing one party to unilaterally vary the agreement removes the element of mutual agreement and consideration, making the variation clause, and potentially the entire agreement, unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Contract consideration — e-lawresources.co.uk 0 Contract consideration In contract law consideration is concerned with the bargain of the contract. A contract is based on an exchange of promises. Each party to a contract must be both a promisor and a promisee . They must each receive a benefit and each suffer a detriment . This benefit or detriment is referred to as consideration . Consideration must be something of value in the eyes of the law - ( Thomas v Thomas) (1842) 2 QB 851. This excludes promises of love and affection, gaming and betting etc. A one sided promise which is not supported by consideration is a gift. The law does not enforce gifts unless they are made by deed. Whilst the common law strictly adheres to the requirement of consideration (although in some instances the courts seem to go to some lengths to invent consideration eg Ward v Byham [1956] 1 WLR 496, Williams v Roffey Bros [1990] 2 WLR 1153) equity will, in some instances, uphold promises which are not supported by consideration through the doctrine of promissory estoppel . Rules of consideration There are various rules governing the law of consideration: 1. The consideration must not be past. 2. The consideration must be sufficient but need not be adequate. 3. The consideration must move from the promisee. 4. An existing public duty will not amount to valid consideration. 5. An existing contractual duty will not amount to valid consideration. 6. Part payment of a debt is not valid consideration for a promise to forego the balance. ~~ Take a quiz on consideration ~~ ~~ Revision activities on contract consideration ~~ ~~ Play the revision arcade games on consideration cases ~~ ~~ Case match drag and drop consideration ~~ ~~ Consideration vortex ~~ ~~ Try a word search on consideration ~~ ~~ Find other games and quizzes on contract law ~~ 1. Consideration must not be past: Re McArdle (1951) Ch 669 Case summary Past consideration may be valid where it was proceeded by a request: Lampleigh v Braithwaite [1615] EWHC KB J17 Case summary 2. Consideration must be sufficient but need not be adequate: There is no requirement that the consideration must be market value, providing something of value is given eg £1 given in exchange for a house would be valid. The courts are not concerned with whether the parties have made a good or bad bargain: Chappell v Nestle [1960] AC 87 Case summary Youtube clip 3. Consideration must move from the promisee If a person other than the promisee is to provide the consideration, the promisee can not enforce the agreement: Tweddle v Atkinson [1861] EWHC QB J57 Case summary 4. An existing public duty will not amount to valid consideration Where a party has a public duty to act, this can not be used as consideration for a new promise: Collins v Godefrey (1831) 1 B & Ad 950 Case summary Unless the promisor goes beyond their duty: Glasbrook Bros v Glamorgan County Council [1925] AC 270 Case summary Ward v Byham [1956] 1 WLR 496 Case summary 5. An existing contractual duty will not amount to valid consideration If a party has an existing contractual duty to do an act, this act can not be used as consideration for a new promise: Stilk v Myrrick [1809] EWHC KB J58 Case summary Unless the party goes beyond their existing duty: Hartley v Ponsonby [1857] 7 EB 872 Case summary or if they confer a practical advantage: Williams v Roffey Bros [1990] 2 WLR 1153 Case summary If the existing contractual duty is owed to a 3rd party this may be used as valid consideration for a new promise: New Zealand Shipping v Satterthwaite [1975] AC 154 Case summary Scotson v Pegg [1861] EWHC Exch J2 Case summary 6. Part payment of a Debt Part payment of a debt is not valid consideration for a promise to release the debt in full: Pinnel's case 1602 5 Rep, 117 Case summary Part payment of a debt is not valid consideration for a promise to forebear the balance unless at the promisor's request part payment is made: a). before the due date b). with a chattel c). to a
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows one party to unilaterally modify the agreement, which contradicts the requirement for mutual agreement and consideration in contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable." By allowing only one party to modify the agreement, the changed text removes the need for mutual agreement, undermining the consideration required from both parties to ensure the contract's enforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 2 also highlights the importance of mutual exchange of promises by stating, "Each party to a contract must be both a promisor and a promisee. They must each receive a benefit and each suffer a detriment."

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall comply with all laws, enactments, regulations, regulatory policies, guidelines and industry codes applicable to them and shall maintain such authorisations and all other approvals, permits and authorities as are required from time to time to perform their obligations under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall comply with all laws, enactments, regulations, regulatory policies, guidelines and industry codes applicable to them, except for those that conflict with this agreement, and shall maintain such authorizations and all other approvals, permits and authorities as are required from time to time to perform their obligations under or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The added exception effectively allows a party to ignore laws that conflict with the agreement. This is fundamentally contrary to the rule of law and good faith. It places the contract above the law, which is legally unsound.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing parties to disregard laws that conflict with the agreement, the clause potentially enables actions that are 'ultra vires,' or beyond the legal power or authority of the parties. It attempts to override existing legal obligations, which is impermissible under the ultra vires doctrine. The contract is subservient to law, not above it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ultra vires | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ultra vires Ultra vires is a Latin phrase, meaning " beyond the powers ." Ultra vires plainly means an action by a company or its agent that exceeds the legal scope of its authority . In this instance, the scope of authority is granted by the company’s own bylaws , constitution , or state statutes under which the company is subject to. For example, a company has set procedures in its bylaws for removing or firing a member of the board. If the company removes a board member without following their bylaws, then the removal is ultra vires . Governments can engage in ultra vires activities as well. When referring to the acts of government bodies (e.g., legislatures), a constitution is most often the measuring stick of the proper scope of power. A real-world example of a company committing ultra vires acts is in the 2015 case, FTC v. Volkswagen Group of America . In this case, Volkswagen was caught reporting manipulated emissions tests for its diesel vehicles. These fraudulent reports were ultra vires acts because they violated environmental requirements and the company’s own requirement of ethical behavior and compliance. As a result of Volkswagen’s ultra vires actions, the company paid billions in settlements. [Last reviewed in February of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex CIVICS COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics business law business organizations wex definitions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction by allowing parties to disregard laws that conflict with the agreement. This directly opposes the fundamental principle that contracts are subservient to the law. The Law Explanation states, 'The contract is subservient to law, not above it.' The modified text tries to place the contract 'above' the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'ultra vires' as an action that 'exceeds the legal scope of its authority.' By permitting parties to ignore laws, the contract enables 'ultra vires' actions, exceeding the legal authority of the contracting parties.
